perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of [COMPANY NAME].
perturbation.explanation: By replacing the Commonwealth of Virginia with a company name (or leaving it blank), the contract attempts to circumvent applicable state laws, including consumer protection laws, which is illegal. This introduces uncertainty about which jurisdiction's laws apply.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification attempts to circumvent the application of the Virginia Consumer Protection Act, which protects consumers from deceptive business practices. By not explicitly adhering to Virginia law, the agreement creates a legal loophole that undermines consumer rights and protections.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces "the laws of the Commonwealth of Virginia" with "the laws of [COMPANY NAME]", effectively removing the application of Virginia law. The Perturbation Explanation states this attempts to circumvent applicable state laws, including consumer protection laws. The Law Explanation specifies the Virginia Consumer Protection Act is undermined. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions the Virginia Consumer Protection Act (Chapter 17 of Title 59.1 of the Code of Virginia) and outlines various consumer protections. By replacing the state law with a company's laws, the contract directly contradicts the intent to protect consumers under Virginia law, creating a clear legal contradiction as it could permit the company to act in ways that violate Virginia's consumer protection laws.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees to protect the Information of the Disclosing Party against unauthorized disclosure and warrants that it applies reasonable safeguards against the unauthorized disclosure Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees to protect the Information of the Disclosing Party against unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the warranty regarding 'reasonable safeguards' weakens the protection of confidential information. Under Virginia law, companies have a duty to implement and maintain reasonable security measures to protect sensitive information. Removing the explicit warranty could be seen as a violation of this duty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia Code § 18.2-186.6. Failure to take reasonable measures to protect personal information.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification eliminates the explicit requirement to implement reasonable safeguards, potentially conflicting with Virginia Code § 18.2-186.6, which pertains to the failure to take reasonable measures to protect personal information, particularly sensitive data. By removing the express commitment to safeguards, the contract weakens the legal obligation to secure disclosed data, thus increasing the potential for non-compliance.

Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 18.2. Crimes and Offenses Generally Chapter 6. Crimes Involving Fraud 6/23/2025 § 18.2-186.6 . Breach of personal information notification. A. As used in this section: "Breach of the security of the system" means the unauthorized access and acquisition of unencrypted and unredacted computerized data that compromises the security or confidentiality of personal information maintained by an individual or entity as part of a database of personal information regarding multiple individuals and that causes, or the individual or entity reasonably believes has caused, or will cause, identity theft or other fraud to any resident of the Commonwealth. Good faith acquisition of personal information by an employee or agent of an individual or entity for the purposes of the individual or entity is not a breach of the security of the system, provided that the personal information is not used for a purpose other than a lawful purpose of the individual or entity or subject to further unauthorized disclosure. "Encrypted" means the transformation of data through the use of an algorithmic process into a form in which there is a low probability of assigning meaning without the use of a confide
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit warranty regarding 'reasonable safeguards,' which the original contract included for the protection of confidential information. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation highlight that this omission may conflict with Virginia Code § 18.2-186.6, as it weakens the legal obligation to secure disclosed data. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing § 18.2-186.6, defines a 'breach of the security of the system' as unauthorized access to unencrypted data. By removing the warranty for reasonable safeguards, the modified contract arguably increases the risk of such a breach and thus contradicts the intention of the law, because it eliminates an explicit commitment to data security measures.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges that in its examination of the Information it will be exposed to material nonpublic information concerning the business and financial condition of the Disclosing Party and consequently the Receiving Party agrees that prior to the date two (2) years from the date hereof, without the prior written approval of the Board of Directors of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party will not (and will insure that its affiliates (and any person acting on behalf of or in concern with the Receiving Party or any affiliate) will not) purchase or otherwise acquire (or enter into any agreement or 2 <PAGE> 3 make any proposal to purchase or otherwise acquire) any securities of the Disclosing Party, any warrant or option to purchase such securities, any security convertible into any such securities or any other right to acquire such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that in its examination of the Information it will be exposed to material nonpublic information concerning the business and financial condition of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the restriction on purchasing securities based on nonpublic information, the agreement could potentially facilitate insider trading, violating federal securities laws. The original clause restricted the receiving party from trading on information gained in the business negotiations, but the modified text removes that restriction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Deleting the clause that prevents the Receiving Party from purchasing securities based on nonpublic information directly contradicts the spirit and intent of securities regulations like Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5, which prohibit the use of material, nonpublic information for trading securities, thus risking insider trading violations.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The original contract included a clause restricting the Receiving Party from purchasing securities of the Disclosing Party based on nonpublic information. The changed text removes this restriction. The Law Explanation states that deleting this clause contradicts securities regulations like Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5, risking insider trading violations. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 78j, concerns manipulative and deceptive devices. While the snippet does not explicitly state 'insider trading,' it prohibits manipulative or deceptive devices in securities trading. The omission of the original clause creates a scenario where the Receiving Party could potentially engage in activities that violate 15 USC 78j, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: At the Disclosing Party's request, all Information of the Disclosing Party in tangible form, or any copies thereof, that is in the possession of the Receiving Party shall be returned to the Disclosing Party or destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: Information of the Disclosing Party in tangible form, or any copies thereof, that is in the possession of the Receiving Party shall be destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the option for the Disclosing Party to request the return of information and mandating only destruction limits the Disclosing Party's control over their confidential information. While destruction ensures confidentiality, it may hinder scenarios where the Disclosing Party needs the information back for legitimate business purposes. Under Virginia's Uniform Trade Secrets Act, trade secret owners have the right to seek return of misappropriated information.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the 'returned to the Disclosing Party' option, the modified text potentially conflicts with the Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act (§ 59.1-338), which allows for injunctive relief, including orders for the return of misappropriated trade secrets. Limiting the remedy to destruction alone could prevent the trade secret owner from regaining possession of their information, especially if return is a more suitable solution.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the Disclosing Party's option to request the return of their information, mandating only destruction. The Law Explanation states that this conflicts with the Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act (§ 59.1-338), which allows for injunctive relief, including orders for the return of misappropriated trade secrets. By limiting the remedy to destruction, the changed text prevents the trade secret owner from regaining possession of their information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 points to Title 59.1 of the Code of Virginia, which governs trade and commerce, thus providing the relevant scope to potentially include the trade secrets act and its remedies. Although the snippet doesn't explicitly detail the trade secret act section, the Perturbation Explanation also highlights that under Virginia's Uniform Trade Secrets Act, trade secret owners have the right to seek return of misappropriated information. This omission directly contradicts the remedies available under the Act, thereby creating a legally relevant contradiction.
